content:
# Numbers Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 152-153 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0114-0115
**Source Images:** `NUM_005_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_005_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH spoke to Moses:

**2** "Command the Sons of Israel to send away from the camp anyone with a skin disease, anyone with a bodily discharge, and anyone who has become ritually contaminated by contact with a dead body.

**3** You must send away both men and women -- put them outside the camp so that they do not contaminate the camp where I dwell among them."

**4** The Sons of Israel did exactly that. They sent such people outside the camp, just as YHWH had told Moses.

**5** YHWH spoke to Moses:

**6** "Say to the Sons of Israel: When a man or woman wrongs another person and so acts unfaithfully against YHWH, that person bears guilt.

**7** They must confess the wrong they have committed. Then they must make full restitution for the wrong, adding one-fifth to the amount and paying it to the person they have wronged.

**8** But if the wronged person has no close relative to whom restitution can be made, the restitution belongs to YHWH and is to be given to the priest, in addition to the ram of purification with which atonement is made for the offender.

**9** Every sacred contribution that the Sons of Israel bring to the priest belongs to the priest.

**10** Each person's sacred offerings are their own to give, but whatever anyone gives to the priest becomes the priest's."

**11** YHWH spoke to Moses:

**12** "Speak to the Sons of Israel and say to them: Suppose a man's wife goes astray and acts unfaithfully toward him --

**13** another man has sexual relations with her, but it is hidden from her husband's eyes. She has defiled herself in secret; there is no witness against her, and she was not caught in the act.

**14** If a spirit of jealousy comes over the husband and he becomes suspicious of his wife -- whether she has actually defiled herself or not --

**15** then the husband is to bring his wife to the priest. He must also bring an offering on her behalf: one-tenth of an ephah of barley flour. He must not pour oil on it or place frankincense on it, because it is a grain offering of jealousy -- an offering of remembrance designed to bring wrongdoing to light.

**16** The priest is to bring her forward and have her stand before YHWH.

**17** Then the priest is to take holy water in a clay vessel, and he is to take some of the dust from the floor of the Dwelling Place and put it into the water.

**18** The priest is to have the woman stand before YHWH, loosen her hair, and place the grain offering of remembrance in her hands -- the grain offering of jealousy. In the priest's own hands will be the bitter water that brings a curse.

**19** Then the priest is to put her under oath and say to her: 'If no other man has slept with you, and if you have not gone astray and defiled yourself while under your husband's authority, may this bitter water that brings a curse do you no harm.

**20** But if you have gone astray while under your husband's authority and have defiled yourself, and some man other than your husband has been intimate with you' --

**21** at this point the priest is to put the woman under the oath of the curse, and he is to say to her -- 'then may YHWH make you an object of cursing and swearing among your people, by causing your thigh to waste away and your abdomen to swell.

**22** May this water that brings a curse enter your body and cause your abdomen to swell and your thigh to waste away.' And the woman is to say: 'So be it. So be it.'

**23** The priest is to write these curses on a scroll and then wash them off into the bitter water.

**24** He is to make the woman drink the bitter water that carries the curse, so that the water bearing the curse will enter her and cause bitter suffering.

**25** The priest is to take the grain offering of jealousy from the woman's hands, elevate the offering before YHWH, and bring it to the altar.

**26** Then the priest is to take a handful of the grain offering as the memorial portion and burn it on the altar. After that, he is to make the woman drink the water.

**27** Once he has made her drink the water, if she has defiled herself and acted unfaithfully toward her husband, then the water that brings a curse will enter her and cause bitter suffering -- her abdomen will swell and her thigh will waste away, and the woman will become an object of cursing among her people.

**28** But if the woman has not defiled herself and is clean, she will be cleared of guilt and will be able to conceive children.

**29** This is the procedure for cases of jealousy, when a woman goes astray and defiles herself while under her husband's authority,

**30** or when a spirit of jealousy comes over a husband and he becomes suspicious of his wife. He is to have the woman stand before YHWH, and the priest is to carry out this entire procedure with her.

**31** The husband will be free from guilt, but the woman will bear the consequences of her wrongdoing."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 2 -- "skin disease"** (Heb. *tsarua*): The Hebrew term *tsara'at* is traditionally rendered "leprosy," but the condition described in Leviticus 13-14 encompasses a range of skin diseases far broader than Hansen's disease. The LXX (Vaticanus) uses *lepros*, which became the basis for the misleading traditional translation. The point of the exclusion is ritual status, not medical quarantine in the modern sense -- though the practical effect overlaps. The three categories (skin disease, discharge, corpse contamination) represent the three major sources of ritual impurity in the Levitical system.

**v. 3 -- "where I dwell among them"** (Heb. *asher ani shokhen be-tokham*): This is the theological center of the expulsion command. The camp must be purified not primarily for hygienic reasons but because YHWH's Presence dwells (*shokhen*) at its center. The verb *shokhen* is the root of *mishkan* (Dwelling Place/Tabernacle) and the later rabbinic concept of *Shekhinah*. Impurity and divine Presence cannot coexist in the same space.

**v. 6-7 -- "wrongs another person... unfaithfully against YHWH"** (Heb. *lim'ol ma'al ba-YHWH*): The Hebrew reveals a profound theological claim: every wrong committed against a fellow human is simultaneously an act of unfaithfulness (*ma'al*) against YHWH. The term *ma'al* specifically denotes breach of trust or misappropriation of what belongs to another -- including what belongs to God. The required confession (*hitvaddu*) is the hithpael form, implying a thorough, reflexive acknowledgment. The twenty-percent surcharge on restitution (*chamishitho*) functions as both penalty and incentive for honesty.

**v. 8 -- "no close relative"** (Heb. *go'el*): The *go'el* is the kinsman-redeemer -- the nearest male relative responsible for protecting family interests. This legal provision addresses the case where the wronged party has died with no surviving heir. Rather than the debt evaporating, it transfers to YHWH himself, represented by the priest. The ram of atonement (*eil ha-kippurim*) accompanies the monetary restitution, covering the spiritual dimension of the offense.

**v. 12-31 -- The ordeal of jealousy** (*sotah*): This passage describes the only trial-by-ordeal procedure in the Torah. The entire ritual is designed to resolve an otherwise unresolvable situation: suspected adultery with no witnesses and no evidence. Several features deserve attention:

**v. 15 -- "barley flour... no oil... no frankincense"**: The offering is deliberately austere. Barley was the cheapest grain (wheat offerings were standard). The absence of oil and frankincense -- which normally accompanied grain offerings (Lev 2:1) -- strips the offering of any festive or pleasing quality. The LXX (Vaticanus: *aleuron krithinon*) confirms the barley specification. This offering is not a gift to be enjoyed but an instrument of investigation -- a *minhah of remembrance* (*mazkeret*), designed to "bring wrongdoing to light" (literally "bring iniquity to remembrance").

**v. 17 -- "holy water... dust from the floor"** (Heb. *mayim qedoshim... afar min ha-qar'qa*): The "holy water" is likely water from the laver used in the sanctuary. The dust from the Dwelling Place floor symbolically infuses the water with the holiness of the sacred space, making it an agent of divine judgment. The clay vessel -- the humblest container -- underscores the sobriety of the proceedings.

**v. 18 -- "loosen her hair"** (Heb. *u-fara et rosh ha-ishah*): The loosening or uncovering of the woman's hair is a gesture of exposure and vulnerability. A married woman's covered hair signified her protected status; its loosening ritually suspends that status during the trial. The LXX renders this *apokaluphsei* ("uncover"), emphasizing the exposure dimension.

**v. 21-22 -- "thigh to waste away and abdomen to swell"** (Heb. *be-nappil YHWH et yerekekh... u-ve-hatzbiah bitnekh*): The precise medical meaning has been debated for millennia. The Hebrew *yarekh* ("thigh") is a known euphemism for the reproductive organs. The "swelling" (*tsavah*) of the abdomen combined with the "wasting" (*naphal*, literally "falling") of the thigh likely describes some form of reproductive dysfunction or miscarriage. The curse targets the capacity for childbearing -- the very domain where the alleged offense occurred. The double *amen* ("So be it. So be it") required from the woman signifies her voluntary submission to the divine verdict.

**v. 23 -- "write these curses on a scroll and wash them off"**: The physical dissolution of the written words into the water is a striking act of sympathetic ritual. The curses become materially present in the liquid the woman drinks. This is one of the rare biblical instances where the written word is treated as a physical substance carrying power -- a concept with deep resonances in ancient Near Eastern legal and magical practice, though the biblical text insists the operative agent is YHWH, not the ritual itself.

**v. 28 -- "will be able to conceive children"** (Heb. *ve-nizre'ah zara*): Literally "she will be sown with seed" -- an agricultural metaphor for conception. If the woman is innocent, the outcome is not merely acquittal but positive fertility. The ordeal is thus not only punitive for the guilty but restorative and vindicating for the innocent. The LXX (Vaticanus: *spermatisthesetai sperma*) preserves the emphatic doubling of the seed root.

**v. 31 -- "The husband will be free from guilt"** (Heb. *ve-niqqah ha-ish me-avon*): This closing provision protects the husband from guilt for bringing a potentially false accusation. It also implicitly constrains him: he cannot act on his jealousy privately or violently. The only legitimate channel for his suspicion is this public, priestly procedure. In a cultural context where suspected adultery could provoke honor killings, the *sotah* ritual functioned as a legal circuit-breaker -- transferring judgment from human rage to divine adjudication.

**Structural note**: Numbers 5 contains three distinct legal sections that share a common theme of purity and communal integrity. The first (vv. 1-4) addresses physical impurity that threatens the camp's sanctity. The second (vv. 5-10) addresses financial wrongs -- a form of social impurity. The third and longest (vv. 11-31) addresses suspected sexual impurity within marriage. The progression moves from the most visible and objective category of defilement to the most hidden and subjective. The chapter thus establishes that maintaining the holiness of YHWH's dwelling community requires vigilance at every level -- bodily, economic, and relational. The Vaticanus LXX text aligns closely with the Masoretic throughout this chapter, with only minor lexical variations, confirming the stability of this legal tradition across the Hebrew and Greek manuscript families.
